coredata.dc:title: Sensitive and rapid detection of fingerprints based on electrospun nanofibrous membranes and quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Hong
Ju, Yanyun
Han, Ting
Ye, Sixia
Zhao, Guanghui
Dong, Lijie
coredata.dc:description: Fingerprints are the unique features of individuals, and the inefficient and time-consuming detection methods limit their wider application in the field of personal information identification. In this study, a new strategy is proposed for effective and rapid detection of fingerprints based on electrospun nanofibrous membranes and quantum dots (QDs). The prepared membranes had nano-scale pores structures and fibers, and the fingerprint residues could stably adhere to the nanofibers through non-specific adsorption to achieve the stability of the fingerprint morphology after the finger being pressed on the membrane. Due to the hydrophobic interactions between QDs and fingerprint residues, bright UV-irradiated emission of fingerprints pattern with precise level 3 details can be recorded after the membrane being immersed in the QDs solution for only 30 s. More importantly, the obtained fingerprints were invisible in sunlight, which is critical for fingerprint encryption. Besides, the results revealed that the latent fingerprints (LFPs) on the surface of different substrates could be transferred by the membranes and be developed by QDs. This study provides a promising method to achieve the precise detection and complete storage of fingerprints information.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Latent fingerprints
Electrospinning
Quantum dots
Fluorescence detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of polyamide-6 (PA6) electrospun nanofibrous membrane
Detection of fingerprints on nanofibrous membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51773163 ), Sanya Science and Education Innovation Park of Wuhan University of Technology (No. 2020KF0025 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (WUT: 2020lll038GX ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Digital photos of the prepared QDs solution under natural light (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right). (b) TEM image of CdSe/CdxZn1−xS QDs dispersed in chloroform. (c) FT-IR spectrum of QDs. (d) UV−vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the QDs.

(a) SEM image of electrospun nanofibers prepared under the environment temperature of 45 °C, (b) The diameters distribution of the nanofibers. (c) FT-IR spectra of PA6 pellet and PA6 nanofibrous membrane. (d) Digital images of electrospinning nanofibrous membranes under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right).
(a) SEM image of the ridge zone of fingerprint on the nanofibrous membrane. (b) Magnified SEM image of the fingerprint residues existing along nanofibers.

Fluorescence images of the fingerprints on the electrospun nanofibrous membrane dip-coated in QDs solution 30 s with a different concentration of (a) 100 μg/mL, (b) 200 μg/mL, (c) 400 μg/mL, (d) 600 μg/mL, (e) 800 μg/mL, (f) 1 mg/mL. (g) Magnified images of red box showing level 2 details including crossover (blue circle), termination (yellow circle) and bifurcation (purple box), and level 3 details (sweat pores in red circle).
The fluorescence images of fingerprint obtained from different substrates of (a) aluminum foil, (b) glass, (c) PE film, (d) wood.

The schematic illustrations of the procedures for fingerprint residues being adhered to nanofibers and the hydrophobic interactions occurring between the long alkyl chains of QDs and fingerprint residues.

head.ce:title: Sensitive and rapid detection of fingerprints based on electrospun nanofibrous membranes and quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Smart Materials and Devices, State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology, Luoshi Road 122, Wuhan 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Smart Materials and Devices, State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Smart Materials and Devices, State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology, Luoshi Road 122, Wuhan 430070, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fingerprints are the unique features of individuals, and the inefficient and time-consuming detection methods limit their wider application in the field of personal information identification. In this study, a new strategy is proposed for effective and rapid detection of fingerprints based on electrospun nanofibrous membranes and quantum dots (QDs). The prepared membranes had nano-scale pores structures and fibers, and the fingerprint residues could stably adhere to the nanofibers through non-specific adsorption to achieve the stability of the fingerprint morphology after the finger being pressed on the membrane. Due to the hydrophobic interactions between QDs and fingerprint residues, bright UV-irradiated emission of fingerprints pattern with precise level 3 details can be recorded after the membrane being immersed in the QDs solution for only 30 s. More importantly, the obtained fingerprints were invisible in sunlight, which is critical for fingerprint encryption. Besides, the results revealed that the latent fingerprints (LFPs) on the surface of different substrates could be transferred by the membranes and be developed by QDs. This study provides a promising method to achieve the precise detection and complete storage of fingerprints information.

ce:section.ce:para: As a unique personal characteristic, fingerprints are widely used in the storage and confirmation of individual information [1]. Especially in the forensic field, it is important to obtain the enough details of fingerprints in a short time. However, fingerprints, as the contact imprints of the friction ridge of finger on the surface of solid substrates, are typically invisible by the naked eyes, which are named as latent fingerprints (LFPs) [2–4]. Thus, proper developing methods need to be employed to achieve the visualization of fingerprints and to acquire clear and sufficient details of fingerprints as soon as possible.

The fingerprint residues are usually mixtures of natural secretions, which contain water, fatty acids, salts, and free amino acids [3]. Various chemical and physical developing methods enhance the contrast between the fingerprint and substrate to visualize the fingerprint, based on the selective interaction between the developing agent and fingerprint residues. Ninhydrin spraying and vacuum metal depositioncan can sensitively detect fingerprints on the surface of porous and non-porous substrates, respectively [5,6]. However, a complex developing progress is usually involved in these methods. In contrast, traditional method of powder dusting such as aluminum powder can achieve the rapid detection effect but have the acute inhalation toxicity [7–9]. Consequently, it is an urgent need to develop efficient and safe materials for precise fingerprint detection.

Fluorescent materials, including organic dyes [10–13], up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNP) [14–17], rare earth complexes [18–21], and QDs [22–25], are frequently used for fingerprint detection as a result of their unique fluorescence characteristics. Among various fluorescent materials, QDs have gained more attentions due to their higher luminescence intensity, stronger photo-bleaching resistance and environmental stability than commercial organic fluorescent dyes [23,26–28]. In recent research, the QDs applied in fingerprint development are designed to be water-soluble to avoid contact toxicity [29,30]. However, water-soluble QDs have a series of limitations, such as terrible environmental stability, reduced fluorescence properties, and inefficiently contacting with the fingerprint residues, all of which seriously affect the accuracy of fingerprints detection [31]. In addition, the sensitive detection of fingerprints is not only limited by the properties of QDs, but also depends on the surface structure of the substrate. The QDs cannot efficiently develop the fingerprints if the fingerprint residues are uncompleted attached to the substrates surfaces. To date, many membrane substrates, such as cellulose membranes and polymer films [32–35], have been used for fingerprint detection by cooperating with different fluorescent developing agents. However, the fingerprint residues usually fail to adhere to the surface of membrane efficiently due to the large size of the fibers and pore structures [29,36,37]. Moreover, the substrates should have no apparent background fluorescence [38]. Therefore, it can be known that the effect of fingerprint detection depends heavily on the surface properties of developing agents and substrates.

In this work, we report a strategy for sensitive and rapid detection of fingerprints based on electrospun nanofibrous membranes and QDs. The large specific surface area, high porosity, and excellent hydrophilicity of nanofibrous membrane can enhance the absorption of fatty acid residues along the nanofibers after fingers being pressed on the membrane. It is only necessary to immerse the membranes containing the fingerprints into the QDs solution for 30 s, while the lipophilic QDs can be tightly combined with fatty acid residues by hydrophobic interactions to develop the fingerprints pattern. Furthermore, we found that LFPs, existing on different substrates such as glass or metal, can be transferred by the prepared membranes. The cooperation of electrospun nanofibrous membranes and QDs provides a new method not only for the secure storage of fingerprint information, but also for the forensic fingerprint enhancement techniques.

Cadmium oxide (CdO, 99.9%), Selenium (Se, 99.5%), Sulfur (S, 99.9%), Trioctylphosphine (TOP, 99.9%), 1-Octadecene (ODE, 90%), Zinc acetate dihydrate (ZnAc2.2 H2O, 99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Chloroform (CHCl3, 99.9%), Oleic acid (OA, 90%), Formic acid (FA, 88%), Polyamide-6 (PA6, Mw=20000) were purchased from China National Medicines Corporation Ltd. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was provided by a Millipore water purification system.

The QDs were prepared according to a typical synthesis procedure with appropriate improvements [39]. 0.2 mmol Se and 4 mmol S dissolved in 2 mL TOP were putted in a reaction tube with full N2 gas in reserve. 0.4 mmol of CdO, 4 mmol of ZnAc2.2 H2O, 17.6 mmol of OA, and 20 mL of ODE were placed in a 100 mL round flask and filled with N2 gas. Heating the mixture to 150 °C and vacuumizing the flask repeatedly to ensure that the air is completely removed. Then, further heating the mixture to 310 °C to form the Cd(OA)2 and Zn(OA)2 solution. At this temperature, the previously prepared Se/S precursor was added to the flask and maintained at 300 °C to form QDs. Then it drops to room temperature rapidly to stop the growth of QDs. The synthesized QDs were purified at least three times by chloroform and acetone. Finally, the purified QDs were dispersed in chloroform in reserve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polyamide-6 (PA6) electrospun nanofibrous membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The pure nanofibrous membranes with large specific surface area and high porosity were fabricated by electrospinning. Before dispensing the spinning solution, PA6 was placed in vacuum drying oven to remove the moisture at 60 °C. The appropriate amount of PA6 pellets were dissolved in formic acid to form the spinning solution with concentrations of 8 wt%. In order to ensure the complete dissolution of PA6, stirring the solution for 24 h at room temperature. The spinning solution was then filled into a 10 mL plastic syringe with a blunt-ended needle (0.6 mm inside diameter). The electrospinning voltage was 12 kV, and the syringe's propulsion rate was controlled at 1.2 mL/h. The distance between the needle and the rotating collector wrapped by aluminum foil was 16 cm. All of the electrospinning processes were carried out at a temperature of 45 °C and a humidity of 30%. Finally, the prepared nanofibrous membranes were stored in a desiccator before use.

In terms of fingerprint storage, to ensure the integrity of the membrane, the aluminum foil acted as supporting material was not separated from the membrane. Before collecting the fingerprints, the fingers of the volunteer should be cleaned up and be dried. Subsequently, volunteers rubbed their fingers gently on the forehead and lightly pressed them on the prepared membrane. The membrane with fingermarks left was submerged in QDs/ chloroform solution for 30 s. Then the membrane was put in the pure chloroform for 3 min to wash redundant quantum dots and was placed in a vacuum oven for 1 h at 60 °C to remove the residual solvent. Under 365 nm UV light, clear fluorescent fingerprint patterns could be observed and recorded by the camera.

In terms of transferring LFPs, the electrospun nanofibrous membrane was pressed onto several substrates’ surface containing fingerprints, and then the membrane with fingerprints was immersed in QDs solution. All subsequent steps were the same as described previously.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained using a JEM-7500F SEM (HITACHI, Japan) at 5 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured on a JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The absorption spectrum was obtained by a UV-3600Plus ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra were recorded on a Nexus Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra was measured by an RF-5301 PC spectrofluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan) with 5 nm bandpass both at excitation and emission. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of QDs was measured by an Empyrean X-ray diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical, Netherlands). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of QDs was recorded on an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The images of fingerprints were recorded with a Sony camera (A5000) under UV light illumination at 365 nm.

The QDs nanoparticles stably dispersed in chloroform were stored in a glass bottle to prevent pollution and evaporation of organic solvents. The QDs solution for the test was diluted from the original colloid solution. Fig. 1a shows that the prepared QDs/ chloroform solution displayed a light yellowish-green color under daylight and emitted bright green fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. The quantum yield of the synthesized QDs was 67%, implying an excellent fluorescence property of QDs, which is a significant characteristic to improve the contrast of fingerprint development. As shown in Fig. 1b, TEM analysis of the morphology provides that the prepared QDs were well dispersed in chloroform solution without agglomeration, which could be resulted from the introduction of oleic acid and trioctylphosphine ligands during synthesis. As shown in Fig. S1, the XRD spectrum illustrates the crystal structure of the synthesized QDs. The XRD pattern of QDs agrees well with the standard diffraction pattern of CdSe and Cd0.22Zn0.78S, which is the standard zinc blende structure. The above evidences prove that the QDs are well prepared. To proving the significant characteristics of the surface composition of the synthesized QDs, the FT-IR spectrum is recorded in Fig. 1c. In the entire area except for the fingerprint region, the peaks barely appeared in 2923 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1 are assigned to -CH2- and -CH3 stretching vibrations.

The infrared absorption spectrum of QDs demonstrated the existence of long alkyl chain ligands. Besides, the XPS spectrum of Fig. S2 provides the explicit characteristics of the surface ligands of QDs. The binding energies of C 1s and O 1s are 283 eV and 530 eV, respectively, which indicate the presence of long alkyl chain ligands. The peaks in 160 eV, 403 eV and 1021 eV represent the binding energies of S 2p, Cd 3d and Zn 2p, respectively, which originate from the shell layer of QDs. It is a critical factor for QDs to develop the fingerprint by hydrophobic interaction. The spectra of UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission are recorded in Fig. 1d. A broad absorption covering the UV region from 280 nm to 450 nm can be observed through the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Meanwhile, strong fluorescence emission spectral line can be observed in the range of 500–580 nm with a peak in 530 nm, which is beyond the typical fluorescence wavelength of the fingerprint [40]. Besides, except for the emission peak in 530 nm, there is no other fluorescence emission peak, suggesting the high quality of the synthesized QDs.

The SEM image of Fig. 2a illustrates the morphology of electrospun nanofibers under the electrospinning voltage of 12 kV, the syringe's propulsion rate of 1.2 mL/h, and the different ambient temperature of 45 °C. The nanofibers diameter distribution ranges from 45 nm to 72 nm with an average diameter of 59 nm, as shown in Fig. 2b. This can be attributed to the solvent volatilizing at an appropriate rate and the jet fully stretching and solidifying under the appropriate ambient temperature. It is well known that PA6, dissolved well in formic acid as an ideal raw material, has excellent thermal stability, chemical resistance, biocompatibility, and environmental degradability. However, although the surface of nanofibers remains smooth, the nanofibers became larger in diameter and wider in size distribution because of the rapid evaporation of the solvent and the insufficient stretching of the nanofibers as the temperature increases further. The smaller size represents a larger specific surface area at the equivalent volume, which undoubtedly facilitates the attachment of fingerprint residues along the nanofibers. Therefore, the choice of electrospun conditions greatly influences the morphology of nanofibers, which is critical to obtain a large specific surface area and high porosity.

To confirm the chemical structure of the electrospun nanofiber membranes, we initially investigated the FT-IR spectra of PA6 pellets and PA6 electrospun nanofiber membrane, as shown in Fig. 2c. The spectra show N-H stretching vibrations at 3300 cm−1, -CH2 asymmetric vibration and symmetrical vibration at 2930 cm−1 and 2860 cm−1, respectively, and broadband of amide bond at 1640 cm−1 and 1540 cm−1. The α crystalline is the most common crystal formation of PA6, and the hydrogen bonds between amide groups in PA6 are antiparallel. However, under the electrospinning condition of high strain rate, the transformation of PA6 crystalline took place. Part of α crystalline transform into γ crystalline, and the hydrogen bonds between amide bonds were twisted and aligned. The α crystalline is not as stable as the γ crystalline due to the intense stretching of the polymer by the electric field during the electrospinning process. Although the crystalline structure of the polymer changed somewhat after spinning, the presence of amide linkages endowed the fibers with excellent hydrophilicity, which provided a basis for the perspiration consisting of the fatty acid residues to be attached along the nanofibers. To further demonstrate the hydrophilicity of the membranes, the contact angle measurements were recorded after the membrane being dried, as shown in Fig. S3. The water contact angle of the membrane was 61°, resulting from the abundant amide linkages of nanofibers. Proper hydrophilicity is beneficial to the extension of perspiration on the nanofibers. Digital photos were recorded to illustrate the macrostates of electrospinning nanofibrous membranes, as shown in Fig. 2d. The sample was photographed directly after preparation without any other management. The prepared membranes were completely white in the day without any defects (Fig. 2d, left), while barely emitted fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2d, right). Non-fluorescence is a significant characteristic for the prepared nanofibrous membrane to be a fingerprint-loaded substrate without background fluorescence, which results in the accurate development of fingerprint.

The SEM images were recorded to further understand the existence status of fingerprints on the nanofibrous membranes in Fig. 3. When the finger was pressed lightly on the membrane, the perspiration consisting of fatty acids, salts, and free amino acids is preserved on the membrane in the form of fingerprint ridges morphology, as shown in Fig. 3a. According to the Fig. 3b, we can clearly observe that the fingerprint residues mostly adhere to the membrane along the nanofibers, which may be ascribed to capillary effect and non-specific adsorption effect [32]. Appropriate nanofiber size and distribution provide opportunities for the attachment and enrichment of fingerprint residues on nanofibrous membranes due to the ideal surface area. More importantly, we chose PA6 with hydrophilicity properties as the electrospinning raw material. The obtained nanofibers can quickly absorb the eccrine sweat and achieve rapid and stable attachment of the remaining sebaceous secretions on the nanofibers to ensure the integrity of the latent fingerprint morphology and provide a basis for subsequent fingerprint development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of fingerprints on nanofibrous membranes
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve efficient detection of fingerprints on electrospun nanofibrous membranes, fingerprint-loaded nanofibrous membranes were immersed in QDs solutions with different concentrations for 30 s, as shown in Fig. 4. The fingerprints emitted green fluorescence under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp after the solvent evaporated. When the concentrations of QDs solution were below 200 μg /mL, the fingerprint morphology was incomplete, and the ridge pattern features of the fingerprint could be only partially observed (Fig. 4a and b) as a result of not enough QDs interacting with the residues at the lower concentration. As the QDs solution concentration increased to 600 μg /mL, the fluorescence fingerprint became clear gradually, and level 2 (crossover, bifurcation, termination) details appear (Fig. 4d). When the concentration of QDs reached 1 mg/mL, the level 3 (sweat pores) details of the fingerprint are clearly observed, which is of great significance to improve the accuracy of fingerprints development (Fig. 4f and g). It is worth noting that the non-fingermark areas are shown as dark areas under ultraviolet lamps due to the short dipping time and the lack of sufficient attachment of QDs. The non-existence of background fluorescence provides more contrast between the substrate surface and fingerprint pattern, leading to the enhancement of LFPs.

Meanwhile, the fluorescence images of fingerprint patterns at different immersing times were recorded, as shown in Fig. S4. When the immersing time was relatively brief 10 s and 20 s, the details of the fingerprints cannot be exhibited completely. On the contrary, QDs were over enriched on the membranes with fingerprint due to the longer immersing times. These results were attributed to the number of QDs attached to the fingerprint residues at different immersing times. Besides, the nanofibrous membranes were prepared on aluminum foil substrates with superior mechanical strength. The membrane could maintain its shape with bending, as shown in Fig. S5, which is an essential basis for preserving fingerprint information on nanofibrous membranes. Most importantly, the fingerprints existing on membranes are invisible under daylight, which is extremely important for the security and encryption of fingerprint information.

In addition to storing and encrypting fingerprint information, we found that the prepared electrospun nanofibrous membrane can transfer fingerprints from different substrates, such as aluminum foil, glass, PE film, and wood. Relatively clear fingerprints detected by QDs can be observed by pressing nanofiber membranes on the areas with fingerprints on different substrates. The fingerprints transferred from the smooth surfaces such as aluminum foil and glass can clearly show the level 2 details, such as endpoints and bifurcations, which have sufficient discrimination to determine the individuality of fingerprints information ( Fig. 5a and b). In contrast, the level 1 details of fingerprints obtained from such rough substrates as wood are observed due to the ineffective adsorption of fingerprint residues (Fig. 5d). The characteristic of the transferring avoids the direct effect to the fingerprint by a fluorescent contrast agent on the substrate and would not destroy the primary fingerprint information.

In terms of fingerprint detection, many fluorescent materials and methods have been used. As shown in Table S1, a comparison of materials, detection process, time, and effect between previous works and this work was carried out. The detection time of this work is 30 s, and the effect can reach level 3 details, which are relatively ideal among these methods. In addition, the immersing method can effectively reduce the toxic effects caused by the method of powder dusting or spraying on the human body.

In this strategy, electrospun nanofibrous membranes are excellent substrates for carrying fingerprints residues, whether the fingerprints are obtained by finger pressing or transferred from the surface of different substrates. After being immersed in QDs solution for only 30 s, fingerprints on the nanofibrous membranes show bright fluorescence under the ultraviolet light irradiation, which can effectively detect the fingerprints.

The efficient carrying of fingerprint residues on nanofibrous membranes benefits from the huge surface area of nanofibers prepared by electrospinning. Perspiration containing various fatty acid residues diffuse rapidly due to the capillary effect after getting in touch with nanofibers. Moisture in sweat evaporates quickly because of the enormous specific surface area and excellent hydrophilicity of PA6 nanofibers, making fatty residues adhere to the nanofibers stably. These properties allow QDs to bind to fatty residues through hydrophobic interactions between the long alkyl chains of QDs and fatty residues, as shown in Scheme 1. Comparison experiments were also designed to illustrate the selective binding of QDs to fatty acid residues, as shown in Fig. S6. The membrane shows no fluorescence after being immersed in QDs for only 30 s. However, the fingerprint pattern obtained by washed-clean finger without other treatments lacks enough details to identify the fingerprint information. As a control experiment, the image of the fingerprint obtained after the finger being rubbed over the forehead (sebum-rich area) was recorded in Fig. S6c. The abundant details of the QDs-adhered fingerprint pattern appeared, which confirm the detection mechanism that the selective interaction between the long alkyl chain of QDs and fatty acid residues through hydrophobic interaction. In order to further observe the existing situation of QDs on the membranes containing fingerprint residues, the morphology image and elemental mapping of the relevant area were recorded. As shown in Fig. S7, QDs were primarily distributed on the area of fingerprint residues along the nanofibers without agglomeration, confirming the selective combination between QDs and fatty acid of fingerprint residues. More importantly, the size of QDs is much smaller than fingerprint residues and nanofibers, which is critical for the fingerprint detection accuracy. Consequently, the sensitive detection of fingerprints can be rapidly obtained on the nanofibrous membranes.

In conclusion, we present a simple method for sensitive and rapid fingerprints detection based on electrospun nanofibrous membranes and QDs. The prepared nanofibers with an average diameter of 59 nm and proper hydrophilicity enable the fingerprint residues in perspiration to adhere to the membranes stably and persistently through capillary effect and non-specific adsorption effects. Besides, the presence of long alkyl chain ligands gives QDs stability while making QDs feasible for fingerprint detection. A study with fingerprint detection showed that the bright fluorescence of fingerprints consisting of exact level 3 details could be observed after the fingerprint-loaded membranes being immersed in the synthesized QDs solution for only 30 s. The selectivity may be attributed to the hydrophobic interactions between QDs and fatty acid residues of fingerprints. Besides, the electrospun nanofibrous membrane can also transfer LFPs from the surface of several substrates. The present strategy may not only provide a new method to detect fingerprints, but also open the door to developing electrospinning technique and fluorescent materials for diverse applications in the fields of secure communication, intelligent devices, and document encryption.

Hong Kang: Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Yanyun Ju: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Ting Han: Writing - review & editing. Sixia Ye: Writing - review & editing. Guanghui Zhao: Supervision. Lijie Dong: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51773163), Sanya Science and Education Innovation Park of Wuhan University of Technology (No. 2020KF0025), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (WUT: 2020lll038GX).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.126716.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots–europium(III) energy transfer architecture embedded in electrospun nanofibrous membranes for fingerprint security and document counterspy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.S. Li, J.H. Liu, T. Yang, P.F. Gao, J. Wang, H. Liu, S.J. Zhen, Y.F. Li, C.Z. Huang, Carbon Quantum Dots–Europium(III) Energy Transfer Architecture Embedded in Electrospun Nanofibrous Membranes for Fingerprint Security and Document Counterspy, Anal. Chem. 91(17) (2019) 11185‐11191. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.9b01936.
P. Hazarika, D.A. Russell, Advances in Fingerprint Analysis, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 51(15) (2012) 3524‐3531. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201104313.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White-emitting carbon dots with long alkyl-chain structure: effective inhibition of aggregation caused quenching effect for label-free imaging of latent fingerprint

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.P. Jiang, Y.X. Yu, X.L. Guo, Z.Y. Ding, B. Zhou, H. Liang, X.C. Shen, White-emitting carbon dots with long alkyl-chain structure: Effective inhibition of aggregation caused quenching effect for label-free imaging of latent fingerprint, Carbon 128 (2018) 12‐20. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.11.070.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reversible response of luminescent terbium(III)-nanocellulose hydrogels to anions for latent fingerprint detection and encryption

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Hai, T.R. Li, J.X. Su, W.S. Liu, Y.M. Ju, B.D. Wang, Y.L. Hou, Reversible Response of Luminescent Terbium(III)-Nanocellulose Hydrogels to Anions for Latent Fingerprint Detection and Encryption, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 57(23) (2018) 6786‐6790. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201800119.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The detection of latent fingermarks on porous surfaces using amino acid sensitive reagents: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Jelly, E.L.T. Patton, C. Lennard, S.W. Lewis, K.F. Lim, The detection of latent fingermarks on porous surfaces using amino acid sensitive reagents: A review, Anal. Chim. Acta 652(1‐2) (2009) 128‐142. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2009.06.023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-containing nanoparticles and nano-structured particles in fingermark detection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A magnetically responsive polydiacetylene precursor for latent fingerprint analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Lee, C.W. Lee, J.M. Kim, A Magnetically Responsive Polydiacetylene Precursor for Latent Fingerprint Analysis, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8(9) (2016) 6245‐6251. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.6b00566.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive development of latent fingerprints using Rhodamine B-diatomaceous earth composites and principle of efficient image enhancement behind their fluorescence characteristics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.J. Yuan, M. Li, M. Wang, X.M. Zhang, Z.H. Yin, K.B. Song, Z. Zhang, Sensitive development of latent fingerprints using Rhodamine B-diatomaceous earth composites and principle of efficient image enhancement behind their fluorescence characteristics, Chemical Engineering Journal 383 (2020) 123076. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.123076.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescence investigation of red-emitting Sr2MgMoO6:Eu3+ phosphor for visualization of latent fingerprint
